agent_output: {"domain_analysis":{"business_context":"DynaPricing is a real-time dynamic pricing system for delivery services that adjusts prices based on demand levels. The CEO's description is high-level and intentionally vague, leaving significant ambiguity around guardrails, thresholds, and automation boundaries.","bounded_context":"Pricing","ubiquitous_language":[{"term":"DynaPricing","definition":"The dynamic pricing product/system that adjusts delivery prices in real time based on demand signals"},{"term":"Demand","definition":"A measure of current delivery request volume relative to capacity — the primary input driving price adjustments"},{"term":"Guardrails","definition":"Upper and/or lower bounds on price adjustments to prevent extreme or irrational pricing outcomes"},{"term":"Base Price","definition":"Implied: the standard delivery price before any dynamic adjustment is applied (not explicitly stated but logically required)"},{"term":"Price Adjustment","definition":"The delta applied to a base price as a function of current demand level"},{"term":"Surge","definition":"A period of high demand triggering upward price adjustment"},{"term":"Demand Signal","definition":"The real-time input data (e.g., order volume, courier availability) used to determine current demand level"}]},"business_rules":[{"id":"BR-1","rule":"Delivery prices must be adjusted in real time based on current demand levels","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"This is the core product policy — a VP could decide to pause dynamic pricing or switch to batch updates. The mechanism is a business decision, not a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given a change in demand level, the system produces a new price within a defined real-time SLA (e.g., seconds, not hours)"},{"id":"BR-2","rule":"When demand is high relative to baseline, prices must increase above the base price","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The direction of the relationship (high demand → higher price) is a business policy. A future strategy could subsidize high-demand periods for market share.","testable_assertion":"Given demand above a defined 'high' threshold, the computed price is strictly greater than the base price"},{"id":"BR-3","rule":"When demand is low relative to baseline, prices must decrease below the base price","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Same reasoning as BR-2 — the inverse relationship is a pricing strategy, not a physical or data constraint.","testable_assertion":"Given demand below a defined 'low' threshold, the computed price is strictly less than the base price"},{"id":"BR-4","rule":"A price must never exceed a defined upper bound (price ceiling guardrail)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"An unbounded price could result in regulatory violations, reputational damage, or charging a customer an absurd amount. Once a ceiling exists, violating it corrupts the integrity of the pricing contract with customers.","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P and configured ceiling C: P <= C must always hold"},{"id":"BR-5","rule":"A price must never fall below a defined lower bound (price floor guardrail)","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A price at or below zero, or below cost, violates fundamental financial integrity. The floor prevents the system from generating economically nonsensical outputs.","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P and configured floor F: P >= F must always hold, and F > 0"},{"id":"BR-6","rule":"The pricing system must operate fully automated without manual intervention for individual price decisions","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"Automation is a product design choice. Ops could introduce manual overrides or approval gates for certain scenarios.","testable_assertion":"Given a demand signal change, the system computes and applies a new price without requiring human approval"},{"id":"BR-7","rule":"A delivery price must always be a positive monetary value","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"A zero or negative delivery price is physically and financially nonsensical — it would mean paying the customer to deliver. This is a data integrity constraint.","testable_assertion":"For any computed price P: P > 0"},{"id":"BR-8","rule":"Price adjustments must be a function of demand — no other unstated factor may unilaterally determine price","invariant":false,"invariant_justification":"The CEO stated demand is the driver, but future iterations will almost certainly incorporate other factors (weather, distance, courier supply). This is a policy constraint of V1.","testable_assertion":"The price computation function takes demand as a required input; removing demand input makes the function invalid"},{"id":"BR-9","rule":"The relationship between demand and price must be monotonically non-decreasing — higher demand must never produce a lower price than lower demand, all else equal","invariant":true,"invariant_justification":"If higher demand produced a lower price, the system would be internally inconsistent and incentivize gaming. This is a logical consistency invariant.","testable_assertion":"Given demand levels D1 > D2 with all other inputs equal, price(D1) >= price(D2)"}],"acceptance_criteria":[{"id":"AC-1","given":"A delivery request arrives during a period of high demand","when":"The system computes the delivery price","then":"The price is higher than the base price, within the configured ceiling"},{"id":"AC-2","given":"A delivery request arrives during a period of low demand","when":"The system computes the delivery price","then":"The price is lower than the base price, at or above the configured floor"},{"id":"AC-3","given":"Demand spikes to an extreme level","when":"The system computes the delivery price","then":"The price does not exceed the configured price ceiling (guardrail holds)"},{"id":"AC-4","given":"Demand drops to near zero","when":"The system computes the delivery price","then":"The price does not fall below the configured price floor and remains positive"},{"id":"AC-5","given":"A demand signal changes","when":"The system processes the new signal","then":"A new price is computed and available in real time without manual intervention"},{"id":"AC-6","given":"Two demand levels D1 > D2 with identical other conditions","when":"Prices are computed for both","then":"Price at D1 is greater than or equal to price at D2"}],"edge_cases":[{"scenario":"Demand is exactly at the boundary between 'high' and 'normal'","expected_behavior":"The system must have a deterministic rule for boundary classification — no oscillation or flapping between price tiers"},{"scenario":"Demand changes extremely rapidly (e.g., event lets out, mass cancellation)","expected_behavior":"The system must handle rapid successive recalculations without producing transient invalid prices; guardrails must hold at every computation step, not just at steady state"},{"scenario":"Two customers request delivery at the same instant during a price transition","expected_behavior":"Each customer receives a price that was valid at their request time; no customer should see a price that was never a valid system output"},{"scenario":"Guardrail ceiling is configured lower than the floor","expected_behavior":"System must reject this as an invalid configuration and refuse to operate rather than produce undefined behavior"},{"scenario":"Demand data source becomes unavailable","expected_behavior":"System must have a fallback strategy — either use last known demand, revert to base price, or halt pricing. It must NOT price with demand=0 by default, as that would trigger minimum pricing incorrectly"},{"scenario":"Price is computed in a currency with minimum denomination (e.g., cents)","expected_behavior":"Final price must be rounded to valid currency precision; rounding must never cause a guardrail violation"},{"scenario":"Base price is updated while dynamic pricing is active","expected_behavior":"The system must atomically apply the new base price to subsequent calculations without producing a price spike or dip that violates guardrails"},{"scenario":"Sustained high demand for days — price stays at ceiling","expected_behavior":"The system continues to re-evaluate on each request; the ceiling is not a 'sticky' state — if demand drops, price must respond accordingly"}],"business_impact":{"financial":"Dynamic pricing directly impacts revenue per delivery. Upside: captures surplus during high demand. Downside: incorrect guardrails could either leave money on the table (ceiling too low) or drive customers to competitors (floor too high). Pricing errors have immediate P&L impact.","operational":"Fully automated system reduces manual pricing overhead but introduces operational dependency on demand data pipelines and real-time computation infrastructure. Monitoring and alerting become critical — a silent pricing bug could run for hours undetected.","user":"Customers will experience variable pricing, which can erode trust if not communicated transparently. Surge pricing is a known friction point (see Uber backlash). The guardrails are essential to maintaining user trust.","risk":"Regulatory risk in jurisdictions with price gouging laws (e.g., during emergencies). Reputational risk if prices appear exploitative. Technical risk of pricing bugs that charge incorrect amounts. Competitive risk if dynamic prices are consistently higher than flat-rate competitors.","stakeholders_affected":[{"group":"Customers","impact":"Directly pay variable prices — may perceive unfairness during surges; may benefit during low demand periods"},{"group":"Couriers/Drivers","impact":"High-demand pricing may attract more couriers to work during surges, improving supply — but only if courier compensation is also dynamic"},{"group":"Finance/Revenue Team","impact":"Must model revenue forecasting with variable pricing — traditional flat-rate models no longer apply"},{"group":"Operations/Support","impact":"Will receive customer complaints about price variability — need clear policies and talking points"},{"group":"Legal/Compliance","impact":"Must review price gouging regulations per jurisdiction; may need price justification audit trails"},{"group":"Product/Marketing","impact":"Must communicate pricing model to customers transparently to maintain trust and reduce churn"}]},"confidence":{"level":35,"reasoning":"The CEO's description is an informal all-hands soundbite, not a product specification. Critical details are undefined: what constitutes 'high' vs 'low' demand, what the guardrail values are, what 'real time' means quantitatively, whether there's a base price concept, what the pricing function shape is, and whether other factors (distance, weight, courier supply) are inputs. The phrase 'probably have some guardrails' indicates even the existence of guardrails is uncertain. This is a vision statement, not a spec.","high_confidence_areas":["Demand drives price direction (up when high, down when low) — explicitly stated","System is automated — explicitly stated","Some form of guardrails will exist — stated with soft commitment"],"low_confidence_areas":["Specific guardrail values (ceiling, floor) — not defined at all","Definition of 'high' and 'low' demand — no thresholds given","Whether a base price exists and how it is determined","The pricing function shape (linear, stepped, exponential)","What 'real time' means (sub-second? per-request? per-minute?)","Whether other factors beyond demand influence pricing","Whether courier compensation is also dynamic","Regulatory compliance requirements by jurisdiction","Whether guardrails are hard stops or soft warnings"],"assumptions":["A base price exists from which adjustments are computed","Guardrails include both a ceiling and a floor","'Real time' means per-request or near-per-request computation","Price must always be positive — cannot pay customers to deliver","The demand-price relationship is monotonically non-decreasing"]},"escalations":[{"type":"ambiguity","description":"The CEO said 'probably have some guardrails' — this is not a commitment. Are guardrails a hard requirement or aspirational? The entire safety model of the system depends on this answer.","affected_stakeholders":["Customers","Legal/Compliance","Finance"],"options":["Guardrails are mandatory hard constraints from day one","Guardrails are a nice-to-have for V2","Guardrails exist but are configurable soft limits with override capability"],"recommendation":"Guardrails must be mandatory hard constraints. Launching dynamic pricing without price bounds is an unacceptable business and regulatory risk. Recommend treating BR-4 and BR-5 as non-negotiable for launch."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No definition of what constitutes 'high' or 'low' demand. Without demand thresholds, the pricing function cannot be implemented or tested.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Product","Finance"],"options":["Define demand as percentile of historical volume","Define demand as ratio of requests to available couriers","Define demand as absolute volume with configurable thresholds"],"recommendation":"Demand should be defined as the ratio of active delivery requests to available courier capacity, as this captures both sides of the supply-demand equation. Product must provide specific threshold values."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No specification of the pricing function — only the direction of adjustment is described. Linear? Stepped tiers? Exponential? This fundamentally changes the financial model.","affected_stakeholders":["Finance","Product","Customers"],"options":["Linear scaling between floor and ceiling","Tiered pricing with discrete demand bands","Continuous curve (e.g., logistic) with configurable parameters"],"recommendation":"Start with tiered pricing (discrete demand bands) for V1 — it is the most explainable to customers and easiest to audit. Request Product to define the tiers."},{"type":"missing_context","description":"No mention of how 'real time' is defined. Sub-second per-request? Batch every N minutes? This drives architecture and infrastructure cost.","affected_stakeholders":["Engineering","Operations","Finance"],"options":["Per-request computation at order time","Pre-computed prices refreshed every N seconds/minutes","Hybrid: pre-computed with per-request override capability"],"recommendation":"Per-request computation is the most accurate but must be evaluated against latency SLAs. Need Product to define acceptable latency for price computation."},{"type":"ambiguity","description":"It is unclear whether dynamic pricing applies to all delivery types or only specific segments (e.g., same-day, express). The CEO said 'delivery prices' generically.","affected_stakeholders":["Product","Customers","Finance"],"options":["Apply to all delivery types uniformly","Apply only to time-sensitive delivery tiers","Apply per delivery type with independent demand signals"],"recommendation":"Clarify with Product. Applying dynamic pricing to standard/economy deliveries may cause more customer backlash than applying it only to premium/express tiers where customers already expect variability."}],"rejection_reasons":[]}
